results.title: AI at ATD26
results.text:
AI at ATD26 

Members save an additional 15% off registration during Member Month! Nonmembers can use code MEMBER2026 at checkout to save 10% off registration.

Register

REGISTER NOW

# AI at ATD26

## AI Education at ATD26

AI is no longer optional in talent development. It is already changing how learning is designed, delivered, personalized, and measured.

In a climate defined by rapid technological change, tighter budgets, and rising performance expectations, learning leaders are being asked to do more than adapt. They are being asked to lead. ATD26 is where they learn how.

ATD26 is designed to meet the needs of learning leaders exactly where they are, whether they are early adopters or still navigating hesitation and risk. The conference features over 35 educational sessions focused on AI, ranging from beginner to advanced applications.

While AI sessions are distributed across the full program, we have two tracks particularly relevant for AI-driven conversations:

Future Readiness

Focused on preparing organizations for emerging needs, this track helps leaders stay curious, informed, and proactive in the face of disruption.

Learning Technologies

Explores how platforms, systems, applications, and tools, including AI learning at scale, shape the future workforce.

Register

## VIEW OUR AI SESSIONS AT ATD26:

## Here are some of the key themes you can expect on AI at ATD26

### AI for Learning Design and Training Innovation

Sessions will focus on how AI is actively reshaping the work of L&D professionals.

Key outcomes include:

- Designing faster, more adaptive learning experiences
- Using AI to support performance at the moment of need
- Scaling content creation without sacrificing quality or relevance

This theme resonates strongly with teams responsible for instructional design, learning experience design, and performance support.

### AI Strategy, Capability, and Organizational Readiness

These sessions focus on leadership decisions surrounding adoption and governance of artificial intelligence.

Key outcomes include:

- Understanding what responsible AI adoption looks like in learning
- Building internal capability, not just purchasing technology
- Navigating governance, change management, and organizational trust

This theme is especially compelling for directors, heads of L&D, and enterprise learning leaders making budget and roadmap decisions.

### AI for Leadership and Workforce Development

AI is changing how leaders coach, develop talent, and build teams. ATD26 has the insights you need to navigate the future of work.

Key outcomes include:

- AI-enabled coaching and leadership development
- Personalized career pathways and onboarding experiences
- Rethinking workforce design in an AI-augmented environment

This theme positions ATD26 as a conference not just about learning tools, but about the future of leadership itself.

## Your In-Person Experience Awaits

Get full access to all sessions,

results.title: AI is changing HR and every CHRO needs help
results.author: The CHRO Office
results.text:
AI is changing HR and every CHRO needs help

# The CHRO Office

SubscribeSign in

# AI is changing HR and every CHRO needs help

### I spent two days at the biggest HR conference of the year. 3 things CHROs need to know:

The CHRO Office

Mar 26, 2026

35

5

Share

READ TIME: 10 MINUTES

I just got back from Transform 2026 in Las Vegas. Two days. Hundreds of CHROs. Panels, keynotes, expo floors, hallway conversations.

Every single conversation was about AI.

Not some of them. All of them. AI had center stage in a way I’ve never seen at an HR event before. And the U.S. Department of Labor used the conference to make a major announcement that affects every company in the country.

But what surprised me most was the gap between what was happening on stage and what people were saying in the hallways.

On stage, vendors were saying AI is easy, straightforward, and transformational. In the hallways, CHROs were telling me it’s messy, overwhelming, and changing so fast they can’t build anything stable.

One CHRO from New York pulled me aside.

She’s actually good with AI. She uses it to analyze employee surveys, build board materials, and consolidate reports from six different payroll vendors across the globe.

Even she said it’s still daunting.

Every time she tries to connect Claude to a folder or set up a workflow, something breaks or doesn’t work the way the tutorial promised.

And she’s one of the advanced ones.

That tension, between what AI promises and what HR actually experiences, was the real story of Transform 2026.

Here are the 3 biggest takeaways.

Let’s dive in.

---

## 1. The U.S. government just launched free AI training for every American worker

This was the biggest announcement of the conference and most people outside the room don’t know about it yet.

https://www.dol.gov/newsroom/releases/osec/osec20260324

Taylor Stockton, Chief Innovation Officer at the U.S. Department of Labor, took the stage and announced “Making America AI Literate,” a national initiative to make sure every American worker has the AI skills to succeed in an AI-driven economy.

That design choice was intentional.

They built it around the reality that most American workers don’t have time to sit through a course. They’re working multiple shifts, commuting, and managing families. So the course meets them where they are: on their phone, on the bus ride home, between shifts.

The course covers five areas:

Understanding how AI works.

Exploring AI use cases beyond just treating it as a search engine.

Learning how to communicate with AI effectively (prompting).

Evaluating AI outputs for accuracy and bias.

How to use AI responsibly.

This matters for CHROs.

The Department of Labor is telling you that AI literacy is now a national workforce priority. If the federal government is training every American on AI basics, your company can’t afford to have an HR team that hasn’t started yet.

And if you’re looking for a free, low-commitment way to get your

results.title: Auto Trader Group PLC (ATDRF) (Half Year 2026) Earnings Call Highlights: Strong Revenue Growth ...
results.author: GuruFocus News      November 6, 2025   3 min read
results.text:
Auto Trader Group PLC (ATDRF) (Half Year 2026) Earnings Call Highlights: Strong Revenue Growth ...

Oops, something went wrong

# Auto Trader Group PLC (ATDRF) (Half Year 2026) Earnings Call Highlights: Strong Revenue Growth ...

AUTO.L

This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Release Date: November 06, 2025

For the complete transcript of the earnings call, please refer to the full earnings call transcript.

### Positive Points

Auto Trader Group PLC (ATDRF) reported a 5% increase in group revenue and a 6% increase in operating profit, indicating strong financial performance.

The company's AI-powered CoDriver product has seen strong engagement, with over 10,000 retailers using the tools, showcasing successful integration of AI into their offerings.

Deal Builder adoption has accelerated, with a significant increase in retailers and listings, enhancing the car buying and selling experience.

Auto Trader Group PLC (ATDRF) maintains a strong market position with over 75% of marketplace activity occurring on their platform, highlighting brand strength.

The company returned 162.2 million to shareholders through share buybacks and dividends, demonstrating a commitment to shareholder value.

### Negative Points

Consumer services revenue decreased by 9%, driven by a decline in private revenue from individual sellers.

The speed of sale remains a challenge, with a one-day quicker sale in October, indicating potential headwinds in inventory turnover.

Marketing spend decreased by 21%, which may impact brand visibility and customer acquisition efforts.

The AutoRama segment reported an operating loss of 1.4 million, although this was an improvement from the previous year.

There is caution around the near-term outlook due to potential volatility in the car market and uncertainties in supply and demand dynamics.

### Q & A Highlights

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 2 Warning Sign with ATDRF.

Is ATDRF fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

Q: Could you help us think through investment requirements in the next 12 to 24 months? Should we interpret your comments as you're well invested and the current formula of flattish margins or slight expansion is the right formula? A: As both Nathan and Katherine mentioned, we've been investing in much of this technology for a long period of time, particularly with our co-driver product. We feel that because these investments have been ongoing, there's no change expected in margin guidance over the next 18 to 24 months. We anticipate consistent margins in the AutoTrader segment, and with AutoRama's profitability improving, group margins could continue to expand. (Respondent: Unidentified_2)

Q: Could you discuss the integration risks and opportunities with chat GPT, and how should we think about the split of economics versus the current status quo? A: We believe structured integrations will be the approach, providing

ATD is the only bidder left. ATD's offer is 20% higher than current Seven and I stock price. ATD has the upper hand and are talking about raising the bid if Seven and I engages? So stock is taking a hit as any higher offer will mean more equity has to be issued to fund the purchase. Lots of other opportunities, especially in Europe and Asia, so not sure why they are so married to doing a deal with Seven and I who are not too interested

results.title: Alphabet to Blow Past Investor Expectations for AI Spending
results.author: Bloomberg Podcasts
results.text:
# Alphabet to Blow Past Investor Expectations for AI Spending | Bloomberg Businessweek

- Channel: Bloomberg Podcasts
- Length: 37:58
- Views: 1.6K
- Likes: 10
- Published: 2026-02-04T15:55:22-08:00
- Category: News & Politics
- Keywords: Businessweek, News, Tech News, Technology, bloomberg Businessweek, bloomberg podcast, business, businessweek podcast, news, news podcast, podcast, podcasts
- Language: en

## Description

The people, companies and trends shaping the global economy.
Watch Carol and Tim LIVE every day on YouTube: http://bit.ly/3vTiACF (http://bit.ly/3vTiACF).
Alphabet Inc. shares slipped after the company reported fourth-quarter revenue that beat expectations but said it plans to spend far more than investors expected in 2026.
The Google parent said it will spend $175 billion to $185 billion this year, compared with the $119.5 billion analysts expected. The company’s fourth-quarter sales, excluding partner payouts, were $97.23 billion, surpassing the $95.2 billion expected on average by analysts, according to data compiled by Bloomberg.
Chief Executive Officer Sundar Pichai said the investments are paying off. “We’re seeing our AI investments and infrastructure drive revenue and growth across the board,” he said Wednesday in the statement. “Search saw more usage than ever before, with AI continuing to drive an expansionary moment.” Google Cloud revenue was $17.7 billion, beating the $16.2 billion analysts expected.
Google has raced to reinvent its business for the AI age, working to keep consumers in the habit of going to its search page even when they could also go to chatbots from rivals like OpenAI. The company has quickly improved its Gemini model and integrated it throughout its products — an effort that has required massive investment in data centers and chips for model improvement and cloud customers.
The industry has leaned on Google’s progress. Google is supplying up to one million of its specialized AI chips to Anthropic, cementing Google’s position as a key infrastructure provider in the AI space. Gemini will also be a provider of AI for Siri on Apple Inc.’s iPhones. The Gemini app has 750 million monthly active users.

Today's show features:
Bloomberg Intelligence Senior Global Head of Technology Research Mandeep Singh and reacts to quarterly earnings from Alphabet
Dan Ives, Global Head of Technology Research at Wedbush Securities, on Alphabet earnings and why he sees the world's biggest software companies weathering the storm brought on by artificial intelligence
Bloomberg Economics Chief Geoeconomics Analyst Jennifer Welch on Wednesday’s call between President Donald Trump and China’s Xi Jinping, and strained talks between the US and Iran
Axel Merk, President and Chief Investment Officer of Merk Investments, on the precious metals trade and the potential market impact of a Kevin Warsh-led Federal Reserve
See omnystudio.com/listener (https://omnystudio.com/listener) for privacy information.


Carol Massar and Tim Sten
